metadata.summary: Data preparation for OpenAI fine-tuning: JSONL validation, token counting, CSV conversion, TrainingDataBuilder, TrainingDataValidator, and dataset quality assessment.

content.title: OpenAI Fine-Tuning Data Preparation
content.summary: High-quality training data is the primary determinant of fine-tuning success. This methodology covers JSONL format requirements, validation procedures, token counting, train/validation splitting, CSV conversion, and reusable Python builders and validators for OpenAI fine-tuning datasets.

why.p: Training data quality is the main failure mode in fine-tuning. A JSONL file with 10% noisy examples degrades the entire run. Malformed JSONL fails only at job start — not at upload — so validation before upload is essential. Token counting before upload prevents cost surprises and ensures examples stay under the 4K per-example limit.
The OpenAI fine-tuning API is strict about format: missing "messages" keys, non-list message arrays, missing "role" or "content" fields all cause job failure with minimal error messages.

list.item: Before every fine-tuning job upload — validate JSONL format and count tokens.
When building training data from CSV, database exports, or human annotations.
When assessing dataset quality: coverage, balance, duplicates, PII exposure.
When generating training examples programmatically from existing documents.
Data is already validated and in correct JSONL format — skip re-validation to save time.
Using the OpenAI Playground dataset builder — it validates format automatically.

section.name: data-requirements
section.p: Minimum data requirements for OpenAI fine-tuning:

list.item: Example length: under 4K tokens each recommended.

rule.statement: Collect 50-100+ high-quality examples before starting fine-tuning. Ensure diversity across edge cases. Include examples of the desired output format. Balance categories if the task is classification.
rule.rationale: With fewer than 50 examples the model memorizes rather than generalizes. Imbalanced categories produce a model biased toward the overrepresented class. Edge case coverage determines out-of-distribution robustness.

section.name: jsonl-validation
section.p: Validate JSONL format before uploading to catch errors early. The validator checks JSON syntax, required keys, message structure, and role presence.

example.title: JSONL validator

section.name: token-counting
section.p: Count tokens before uploading to estimate training cost and verify per-example limits.

example.title: Token counter with cost estimation
example.code:
import tiktoken import json
def count_tokens(filepath: str, model: str = "gpt-4o") -> dict: """Count tokens in training file."""
encoding = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
total_tokens = 0 example_count = 0 token_counts = []
with open(filepath, 'r') as f: for line in f: data = json.loads(line) example_tokens = 0
for msg in data["messages"]: # Count message tokens example_tokens += len(encoding.encode(msg.get("content", ""))) # Add overhead for role and formatting example_tokens += 4
example_tokens += 2 # Conversation overhead token_counts.append(example_tokens) total_tokens += example_tokens example_count += 1
return { "total_tokens": total_tokens, "example_count": example_count, "avg_tokens_per_example": total_tokens / example_count, "max_tokens": max(token_counts), "min_tokens": min(token_counts) }
# Training cost estimator def estimate_training_cost( jsonl_path: str, model: str = "gpt-4.1-mini", epochs: int = 3 ) -> float: prices = {"gpt-4.1": 25, "gpt-4.1-mini": 3, "gpt-4.1-nano": 1} # $ per 1M tokens enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model.replace("gpt-4.1", "gpt-4o")) total_tokens = 0 with open(jsonl_path) as f: for line in f: row = json.loads(line) for msg in row["messages"]: total_tokens += len(enc.encode(msg["content"])) cost = (total_tokens / 1_000_000) * prices.get(model, 3) * epochs print(f"Tokens: {total_tokens:,} | Epochs: {epochs} | Est. cost: ${cost:.2f}") return cost
# Usage stats = count_tokens("training.jsonl") print(f"Total tokens: {stats['total_tokens']:,}") print(f"Estimated cost (3 epochs, gpt-4o-mini): ${stats['total_tokens'] * 3 * 3 / 1_000_000:.2f}")

section.name: csv-conversion
section.p: Convert tabular data (CSV with input/output columns) to JSONL format for fine-tuning.

example.title: CSV to JSONL converter
example.code:
import csv import json
def csv_to_jsonl( csv_path: str, jsonl_path: str, system_prompt: str, input_col: str, output_col: str ): """Convert CSV with input/output columns to JSONL."""
with open(csv_path, 'r') as csv_file, \ open(jsonl_path, 'w') as jsonl_file:
reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file)
for row in reader: example = { "messages": [ {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt}, {"role": "user", "content": row[input_col]}, {"role": "assistant", "content": row[output_col]} ] } jsonl_file.write(json.dumps(example) + "\n")
# Usage csv_to_jsonl( csv_path="data.csv", jsonl_path="training.jsonl", system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.", input_col="question", output_col="answer" )

section.name: training-data-builder
section.p: The TrainingDataBuilder class provides a fluent API for constructing SFT and DPO training datasets programmatically.

example.title: TrainingDataBuilder — fluent API
example.code:
from typing import List, Dict, Optional import json
class TrainingDataBuilder: """Build training data in correct format."""
def __init__(self, system_prompt: Optional[str] = None): self.system_prompt = system_prompt self.examples: List[Dict] = []
def add_example( self, user_message: str, assistant_response: str, system_prompt: Optional[str] = None ) -> "TrainingDataBuilder": """Add single-turn example."""
messages = []
sys_prompt = system_prompt or self.system_prompt if sys_prompt: messages.append({"role": "system", "content": sys_prompt})
messages.extend([ {"role": "user", "content": user_message}, {"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_response} ])
self.examples.append({"messages": messages}) return self
def add_conversation( self, turns: List[tuple], # [(user, assistant), ...] system_prompt: Optional[str] = None ) -> "TrainingDataBuilder": """Add multi-turn conversation."""
messages = []
sys_prompt = system_prompt or self.system_prompt if sys_prompt: messages.append({"role": "system", "content": sys_prompt})
for user_msg, assistant_msg in turns: messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg}) messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_msg})
self.examples.append({"messages": messages}) return self
def add_dpo_example( self, user_message: str, preferred: str, non_preferred: str ) -> "TrainingDataBuilder": """Add DPO preference pair."""
self.examples.append({ "input": [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}], "preferred_output": [{"role": "assistant", "content": preferred}], "non_preferred_output": [{"role": "assistant", "content": non_preferred}] }) return self
def save(self, filepath: str): """Save to JSONL file.""" with open(filepath, 'w') as f: for example in self.examples: f.write(json.dumps(example) + "\n")
def get_examples(self) -> List[Dict]: return self.examples.copy()
# Usage builder = TrainingDataBuilder(system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.") builder.add_example("Hello", "Hi! How can I help you today?") builder.add_example("What's 2+2?", "2 + 2 equals 4.") builder.save("training.jsonl")

section.name: training-data-validator
section.p: The TrainingDataValidator class provides comprehensive format and quality validation with structured results.

example.title: TrainingDataValidator — full validation

section.name: llm-prompts-data-gen
section.p: LLM prompts for generating and improving training examples:

section.name: quality-checklist
section.p: Pre-upload quality checks for training data:

list.item: Consistent format across all examples (same message structure, same system prompt pattern).
No contradictory examples — conflicting input-output pairs cause random model behavior.
Balanced category distribution — for classification tasks.
Examples cover edge cases — test boundary conditions explicitly.
System prompts are consistent — use identical wording across all examples.
No PII or sensitive data — check name, email, phone, SSN patterns before uploading.
No duplicate examples — duplicates bias the model toward memorizing those examples.

antipattern.description: Uploading training data without format validation.
antipattern.reason: Malformed JSONL fails only at job start, not at upload. A job that runs for 30 minutes before failing on a malformed line wastes API time and budget. Always validate locally before uploading.

antipattern.description: Including PII in training data without a Data Processing Agreement.
antipattern.reason: OpenAI processes training data on their infrastructure. Uploading data containing personal information (names, emails, health records) without a DPA and explicit user consent violates GDPR and may violate the OpenAI Terms of Service. Scrub PII before uploading.

antipattern.description: Using inconsistent system prompts across training examples.
antipattern.reason: Inconsistent system prompts teach the model conflicting behavior baselines. The fine-tuned model learns to respond differently based on minor system prompt variations, producing unpredictable production behavior. Use identical wording for all examples.
